worth_it_explanation: Samba TV reports a cross-screen reach number that means different things depending on which channels are in the media plan. Linear TV and connected TV (CTV) app exposure on opt-in smart TVs are observed directly through ACR on the TV glass. Digital exposure on desktop and mobile, and exposure inside walled-garden environments, is handled differently: some depends on connected or probabilistic matching, and some is outside ACR scope entirely. When no one on the team has mapped which channels fall into which category, the cross-screen total gets read as a full census of the campaign's reach. These checks confirm that the team understands what Samba is measuring and how, so the numbers in the report are interpreted correctly and planning decisions rest on an accurate picture of coverage.
intro: Samba TV measures audience exposure across screens using Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data from opt-in smart TVs. ACR on the TV glass observes linear TV and CTV app content directly. Coverage on digital screens (desktop, mobile) and in walled-garden environments works differently: some relies on connected exposure data through Samba's identity graph, and some channels fall outside ACR scope altogether. These checks audit whether the team understands the measurement boundaries for each channel in the media plan, whether channels out of ACR scope are accounted for in cross-screen reporting, and whether the cross-screen reach total in the report is read against an accurate understanding of what was and was not observed.

audit_checks.title: The team does not know which channels Samba measures via ACR versus connected data versus out of scope
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the team does not know how Samba measures each channel in the plan, the cross-screen report is used as if all channels are measured equally. Decisions about budget allocation, reach targets, and frequency management are made on numbers that may represent very different levels of observational confidence by screen.

educate.base:
Samba TV builds its cross-screen measurement on Automatic Content Recognition (ACR), a technology embedded in opt-in smart TVs that reads content on the TV glass, regardless of the source: broadcast, cable, streaming app, or antenna. For linear TV and CTV app content viewed on an opt-in smart TV, ACR produces a direct observation of exposure.

Digital exposure on desktop and mobile is a different situation. Samba does not have ACR data from desktop monitors or mobile screens. Coverage on those channels depends on Samba's identity graph, which connects TV households to digital identifiers through deterministic and probabilistic matching. The quality and completeness of that connection varies by household and by the availability of matching signals. It is not the same as a direct ACR observation.

Walled-garden environments, such as major social platforms or streaming services that do not share impression-level data with third parties, may be partially or fully outside what Samba can observe, depending on any data partnerships in place at the time of the campaign. The Samba TV account team is the right source for current information on which specific walled gardens have active data integrations.

These distinctions matter when reading a cross-screen report. A reach number that combines ACR-observed TV households with identity-graph-matched digital users is not a single homogeneous count. The confidence level differs by channel, and the total should be read with that context in place.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The team has not received or reviewed documentation from Samba TV explaining how each channel in the plan is measured
  • The post-campaign report presents a single cross-screen reach figure with no breakdown by measurement method or channel confidence level
  • The plan includes desktop, mobile, or walled-garden inventory, and the team is not aware of whether Samba has active coverage for those channels
You may want to request a channel-by-channel coverage brief from the Samba TV account team before the campaign launches, so the measurement boundaries are documented before the report is delivered.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a campaign across linear TV, CTV apps, and digital display, the Samba cross-screen reach total may combine ACR-observed TV households with identity-graph-matched digital users. You may want to confirm which portion of the reach figure reflects direct ACR observation versus connected matching so the number is used appropriately in post-campaign analysis.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running awareness across TV and digital, understanding which channels Samba measures via ACR versus connected data will affect how the cross-screen report is used to inform budget decisions. You may want to request channel-level coverage documentation from the Samba TV team before the flight begins.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Cross-Media Audience Measurement Standards: Phase I Video

investigate.intro: Review the campaign plan alongside any documentation from Samba TV on channel coverage, and confirm what the team knows about how each channel in the plan is measured.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV campaign report and identify every channel listed: linear TV, CTV apps, digital display, mobile, and any walled-garden environments.

For each channel, note whether the report includes a footnote, methodology section, or separate documentation explaining whether that channel is measured via ACR on the TV glass, via connected or identity-graph matching, or is outside Samba's measurement scope for this campaign.

Ask the account team or review any pre-campaign documentation to confirm the measurement method for digital and mobile channels specifically. Note whether any walled-garden environments in the plan have a current Samba data partnership.
Record for each channel: the measurement method documented, or 'undocumented' if no explanation was provided.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the measurement method for each channel in the plan as documented by Samba TV. A campaign where every channel has a documented measurement method (ACR-observed, connected-data, or out-of-scope) passes. A campaign where any channel's measurement method is undocumented, or where the team cannot distinguish ACR-observed channels from identity-graph-matched channels, is a fail, recorded with the undocumented channels listed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measurement method for each channel in the plan above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the team has not documented how Samba TV measures each channel in the {{site_name}} media plan. Samba observes linear TV and CTV app exposure directly through Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) on opt-in smart TV screens, while digital and mobile coverage depends on identity-graph matching, and some walled-garden environments may be outside ACR scope entirely. Without that breakdown, the cross-screen reach total is read as a single homogeneous count when it combines channels measured with different levels of observational confidence. {{voice_recommend}} requesting a channel-by-channel measurement methodology brief from the Samba TV account team before the next campaign launches and keeping that documentation alongside the post-campaign report.

audit_checks.title: Channels outside Samba's measurement scope appear absent from cross-screen reach without explanation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a channel in the media plan is outside what Samba can observe, it does not appear in the cross-screen reach report. If the team reads that absence as zero exposure rather than unmeasured exposure, the post-campaign analysis treats an unmeasured channel as a non-performing channel, which can lead to misdirected optimization and inaccurate reach summaries.

educate.base:
A cross-screen measurement report from Samba TV covers the channels it can observe: linear TV and CTV apps on opt-in smart TVs via Automatic Content Recognition (ACR), and digital channels to the extent that Samba's identity graph can connect TV households to digital identifiers. Channels the plan includes but Samba cannot observe will not appear in the report at all. That absence is a coverage gap, not a performance result.

The practical consequence is that a media plan running across four channels may produce a Samba report that covers two of them well and omits the other two entirely. A team reading the report without knowing about the omissions will interpret the cross-screen reach as the campaign's total reach, when it is actually the reach across the measurable subset of the plan.

This matters most when the unmeasured channels represent a meaningful share of the budget. A campaign where 40 percent of spend went to a channel Samba cannot observe is presenting 60 percent of the campaign's exposure as if it were 100 percent. Reach estimates, frequency distributions, and overlap analyses drawn from that report will not reflect the full campaign.

The Media Rating Council (MRC) cross-media measurement standards describe the conditions under which cross-media reach should be reported and the limitations that must be disclosed when measurement coverage is incomplete. Those standards provide a reference point for what a complete cross-screen measurement report should include and disclose.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The media plan includes channels or platforms that the Samba TV account team has not confirmed as within scope for this campaign
  • The cross-screen report does not include a coverage or methodology section listing which channels are included and which are excluded
  • Spend on channels absent from the Samba report represents more than a small share of the overall budget
You may want to confirm before the campaign launches which channels in the plan Samba will and will not report on, so that absence in the report is anticipated and documented rather than discovered after the fact.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer with spend across linear TV, CTV, digital video, and social, a Samba report that covers only linear TV and CTV may account for a fraction of total campaign spend. You may want to confirm the share of budget represented by channels in scope before using the Samba cross-screen total as the reach figure for the full campaign.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running awareness across several channels, a cross-screen reach figure that excludes one or more channels will understate total reach and may make the campaign appear less efficient than it was. You may want to document which channels are in and out of scope before reading reach efficiency metrics from the report.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Cross-Media Audience Measurement Standards: Phase I Video
OTT/CTV and SSAI Digital Video Measurement Guidelines

investigate.intro: Map the media plan against what Samba TV confirms as within scope for this campaign, and note any channels present in the plan but absent from the report.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Pull the finalized media plan and list every channel by name: linear TV networks, CTV app names, digital display, mobile, social platforms, and any other inventory sources.
Open the Samba TV cross-screen report and identify every channel for which data appears. Compare this list against the media plan.

For each channel in the plan that does not appear in the Samba report, confirm with the account team whether the absence is because the channel is out of scope for Samba measurement, or because the campaign did not deliver impressions on that channel.
Calculate the share of total campaign spend represented by channels absent from the Samba report. Record this percentage alongside the channel names.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each channel in the media plan, whether it appears in the Samba report, and the share of budget it represents. A campaign where all channels in the plan appear in the report, or where channels absent from the report are confirmed as out of scope and represent a small share of budget that has been disclosed, passes. A campaign where channels representing a meaningful share of budget are absent from the report with no documented explanation is a fail, recorded with the channel names and the budget share they represent.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the channels present in the media plan, the channels present in the Samba report, and the budget share of any absent channels above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the {{site_name}} media plan includes channels that do not appear in the Samba TV cross-screen report, with no documentation explaining whether those channels are outside Samba's measurement scope or simply did not deliver. When a channel with meaningful spend is absent from a cross-screen measurement report without disclosure, the reach total presented covers only the measurable portion of the campaign, not the full campaign. {{voice_recommend}} mapping every channel in the plan against Samba's confirmed scope before each campaign launches, documenting any channels that will not be reported on, and including that disclosure alongside the cross-screen reach figure in post-campaign analysis.

audit_checks.title: The cross-screen reach total is used as the campaign's full reach when one or more screens are unmeasured
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reading a partial measurement as a complete one produces reach estimates, cost-per-reach figures, and frequency analyses that do not represent the full campaign. Planning and optimization decisions built on those figures carry forward the error into the next campaign.

educate.base:
A cross-screen reach figure from Samba TV reflects the audience Samba was able to observe across the channels it measured for the campaign. If the media plan ran across channels that are outside Samba's measurement scope, the cross-screen total represents a subset of the campaign's actual reach. Using that subset as if it were the full campaign reach produces incorrect cost-per-reach calculations, incorrect frequency distributions, and an inaccurate basis for comparing this campaign against others that used different measurement providers or had fuller coverage.

This is not a critique of Samba TV's accuracy within the channels it covers. It is a question of whether the practitioner is aware of the scope boundary when reading the total. A cross-screen reach figure labeled '12.4 million unique households' is accurate for the channels Samba measured. If two of the plan's five channels were outside scope, it is not the reach figure for the full campaign.

The downstream effects appear in planning documents. If the cost-per-reach target for the next campaign is set using a denominator that excluded two channels of the prior campaign, the target will be set against an incomplete baseline. If the reach overlap analysis excludes those same channels, the deduplication model will not reflect actual audience overlap across the full plan.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The cross-screen reach total from the Samba report is used directly in a cost-per-reach or cost-per-point calculation without a note about which channels are included
  • Post-campaign decks or summaries present the Samba reach figure as the campaign's total reach without qualification
  • The prior-campaign reach baseline used for planning the next campaign came from a Samba report with a different channel mix than the campaign being planned
You may want to add a coverage note to any post-campaign report that includes a Samba cross-screen reach figure, describing which channels are included in the total and which are not, so the number is used correctly in future planning.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer using Samba cross-screen reach to set cost-per-reach benchmarks, you may want to confirm that the channels included in the Samba measurement scope are consistent across campaigns before comparing cost-per-reach figures across flights.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using the Samba cross-screen reach figure to size a retargeting audience or plan the next flight, you may want to confirm that the reach figure reflects all channels in the plan, or that it is labeled as the reach across measured channels only.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Cross-Media Audience Measurement Standards: Phase I Video

investigate.intro: Confirm how the Samba cross-screen reach total is used in post-campaign reporting and planning, and whether any disclosure of measurement scope accompanies it.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the most recent post-campaign report or planning document that references the Samba cross-screen reach figure.
Check whether the report includes a footnote, methodology note, or channel inclusion list alongside the cross-screen total. Note whether any channels are described as excluded from the measurement.

Review how the cross-screen total is labeled: as 'total campaign reach', 'measured reach', 'Samba-measured reach', or a similar qualifier. Note whether the label signals that the figure covers a subset of channels.

Check whether cost-per-reach or cost-per-point calculations use the Samba total as the denominator. If so, note whether those calculations include a disclosure about what is included in the denominator.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner reviews how the Samba cross-screen reach total is used in post-campaign reporting and planning. A report that includes a clear disclosure of which channels are in the measurement scope, and that labels cost-per-reach calculations accordingly, passes. A report that presents the Samba total as the campaign's full reach without qualification, or that uses it in cost-per-reach calculations without noting the channel scope, is a fail, recorded with the specific uses found and whether any channels were excluded from the total.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record how the Samba cross-screen reach total is used in reporting and planning documents, and whether any coverage disclosure accompanies it, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s post-campaign reporting uses the Samba TV cross-screen reach total as the campaign's full reach figure without disclosing which channels are included in the measurement scope. Samba TV measures linear TV and CTV app exposure via Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) on opt-in smart TVs; coverage on digital and walled-garden channels depends on connected data or may be outside ACR scope. When a partial measurement is presented as a complete one, cost-per-reach benchmarks, frequency analyses, and audience sizing for subsequent campaigns rest on an incomplete baseline. {{voice_recommend}} adding a channel-scope disclosure to every post-campaign report that includes a Samba cross-screen reach figure, and labeling cost-per-reach calculations to reflect which channels the denominator includes.
